title: Cliff Tokens: Identifying Single-Token Failure Triggers in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
abstract: arXiv:2606.25524v2 Announce Type: replace  Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) reach high accuracy in mathematical reasoning, but individual traces on the same problem diverge; some arrive at the correct answer while others fail. Prior work analyzes failure at the step, chunk, or sentence level, or at tokens where failure has already occurred. Neither identifies the precise token that triggers the shift toward failure. We introduce the cliff token, a token where the token-wise potential drops significantly under an adaptive threshold that scales with the local token-wise potential, based on a one-sided two-proportion z-test. Across seven models and three mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM1K, MATH500, AIME 2025), cliff tokens act as failure triggers; deleting the first cliff token and resampling recovers pass@64 to 1.0, while keeping it limits recovery to between 0.71 and 1.00. We further introduce a cliff taxonomy of deterministic, uncertain, and sampled-off cliffs, defined by g

Title: Cliff Tokens: Identifying Single-Token Failure Triggers in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
Abstract: arXiv:2606.25524v2 Announce Type: replace  Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) reach high accuracy in mathematical reasoning, but individual traces on the same problem diverge; some arrive at the correct answer while others fail. Prior work analyzes failure at the step, chunk, or sentence level, or at tokens where failure has already occurred. Neither identifies the precise token that triggers the shift toward failure. We introduce the cliff token, a token where the token-wise potential drops significantly under an adaptive threshold that scales with the local token-wise potential, based on a one-sided two-proportion z-test. Across seven models and three mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM1K, MATH500, AIME 2025), cliff tokens act as failure triggers; deleting the first cliff token and resampling recovers pass@64 to 1.0, while keeping it limits recovery to between 0.71 and 1.00. We further introduce a cliff taxonomy of deterministic, uncertain, and sampled-off cliffs, defined by g
translated_title: 悬崖标记：识别大语言模型数学推理中的单标记失败触发点
translated_abstract: arXiv:2606.25524v2 公告类型：替换 摘要：大语言模型在数学推理中达到高准确率，但同一问题的个体推理轨迹存在分歧；有些得到正确答案，而另一些则失败。先前的工作在步骤、片段或句子层面分析失败，或者关注已经发生失败的标记。这些方法都无法识别出触发转向失败的确切标记。我们引入了“悬崖标记”的概念，即基于单侧双比例z检验，在根据局部标记潜力自适应调整的阈值下，标记潜力显著下降的标记。在七个模型和三个数学推理基准（GSM1K、MATH500、AIME 2025）上，悬崖标记作为失败触发点；删除第一个悬崖标记并重新采样可将pass@64恢复到1.0，而保留它则限制恢复率在0.71到1.00之间。我们进一步提出了一个由g定义的悬崖分类法，包括确定性悬崖、不确定性悬崖和采样偏离悬崖。
tldr: 本文提出“悬崖标记”概念，通过识别大语言模型数学推理中单个标记级别的失败触发点，并证明删除该标记可显著恢复模型正确率。
en_tdlr: This paper introduces the concept of "cliff tokens," identifying single-token-level failure triggers in LLM mathematical reasoning, and demonstrates that deleting such tokens can significantly recover model accuracy.
